fast-slow-pointers.title: Fast & Slow Pointers
fast-slow-pointers.description: Deploy two pointers moving at different speeds to detect cycles, find midpoints, and identify structural properties in O(n) time with O(1) space.

middle-of-the-linked-list.title: Middle of the Linked List
middle-of-the-linked-list.description: Given the head of a singly linked list, return the middle node of the linked list. If there are two middle nodes, return the second middle node.

examples.explanation: The middle node is node 3.
There are two middle nodes (3 and 4), return the second one.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [1, 100].

approaches.title: Brute Force — Count Length First
approaches.intuition: First compute the total length of the linked list. Then traverse again to reach the middle index.
steps: Traverse list to compute length.
Compute middle index = floor(length / 2).
Traverse again to that position.

approaches.title: Improved — Store Nodes in Array
approaches.intuition: Store all nodes in an array. Then return the element at index floor(n/2).
steps: Traverse list and push nodes into array.
Return array[Math.floor(array.length / 2)].

approaches.title: Optimal — Two Pointers (Slow & Fast)
approaches.intuition: Use two pointers. Move slow by one step and fast by two steps. When fast reaches the end, slow will be at the middle.
steps: Initialize slow and fast to head.
Move slow by 1 step and fast by 2 steps.
When fast reaches null, slow is at middle.

intersection-of-two-linked-lists.title: Intersection of Two Linked Lists
intersection-of-two-linked-lists.description: Given the heads of two singly linked lists headA and headB, return the node at which the two lists intersect. If the two linked lists have no intersection, return null.

examples.explanation: The two lists intersect at node with value 8.
The two lists do not intersect.

constraints: The number of nodes in each list is in the range [0, 3 * 10⁴].
The lists are guaranteed to have no cycles.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Compare Every Node
approaches.intuition: For each node in list A, check every node in list B to see if they are the same reference.
steps: Traverse each node in list A.
For each node, traverse entire list B.
If nodes match by reference, return that node.

approaches.title: Improved — Hash Set
approaches.intuition: Store all nodes of list A in a set. Then traverse list B and check if any node exists in the set.
steps: Traverse list A and add nodes to set.
Traverse list B.
If a node exists in set, return it.

approaches.title: Improved — Length Difference Technique
approaches.intuition: If the lists intersect, they share the same tail. Compute lengths, advance the longer list by the length difference, then move both together.
steps: Compute length of both lists.
Advance pointer of longer list by difference.
Move both pointers one step at a time.
Return intersection if found.

approaches.title: Optimal — Two-Pointer Switching Technique
approaches.intuition: Traverse both lists with two pointers. When one pointer reaches the end, redirect it to the other list’s head. After at most two passes, they will meet at intersection or null.
steps: Initialize two pointers at headA and headB.
Move both forward one step.
If a pointer reaches null, redirect it to the other list.
They will meet at intersection or null.

linked-list-cycle.title: Linked List Cycle
linked-list-cycle.description: Given head, the head of a linked list, determine if the linked list has a cycle in it. Return true if there is a cycle, otherwise return false.

examples.explanation: There is a cycle in the linked list, where the tail connects to the node at index 1.
The tail connects to the head node, forming a cycle.
There is no cycle in the linked list.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 10⁴].
pos is -1 or a valid index in the linked list.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Hash Set
approaches.intuition: Traverse the list and store each visited node in a set. If we encounter a node that already exists in the set, a cycle exists.
steps: Initialize empty set.
Traverse linked list.
If node already in set → return true.
Else add node to set.

approaches.title: Improved — Modify Node Values
approaches.intuition: Mark visited nodes by modifying their values. If a marked node is encountered again, a cycle exists.
steps: Traverse list.
If node.val is already marked → cycle exists.
Else mark node.val.
cons: Not allowed in many interview scenarios

approaches.title: Optimal — Two-Pointer (Floyd’s Cycle Detection)
approaches.intuition: Use two pointers: slow moves one step, fast moves two steps. If there is a cycle, they will eventually meet.
steps: Initialize slow and fast to head.
Move slow by 1 and fast by 2.
If they meet → cycle exists.
If fast reaches null → no cycle.

breadcrumb: Remove Nth Node From End of List
remove-nth-node-from-end-of-list.title: Remove Nth Node From End of List
remove-nth-node-from-end-of-list.description: Given the head of a linked list, remove the nth node from the end of the list and return its head.

examples.explanation: The 2nd node from the end is 4, so we remove it.
Removing the only node results in an empty list.
The last node is removed.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is sz.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Compute Length First
approaches.intuition: First compute the total length of the list. The node to remove is at position (length - n) from the start.
steps: Traverse list to compute length.
Compute indexToRemove = length - n.
Traverse again to node before that index.
Adjust pointers to skip target node.

approaches.title: Optimal — Two Pointers (One Pass)
approaches.intuition: Use two pointers with a gap of n nodes. Move fast pointer n steps ahead. Then move both pointers together until fast reaches the end. Slow will be right before the node to remove.
steps: Create a dummy node pointing to head (handles edge cases).
Move fast pointer n steps ahead.
Move both fast and slow until fast reaches end.
Remove slow.next node.
pros: Handles head removal cleanly with dummy node

odd-even-linked-list.title: Odd Even Linked List
odd-even-linked-list.description: Given the head of a singly linked list, group all nodes with odd indices together followed by the nodes with even indices, and return the reordered list. The relative order inside the odd and even groups must remain the same.

examples.explanation: Odd index nodes: 1,3,5. Even index nodes: 2,4.
Nodes are rearranged preserving internal order.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 10⁴].

approaches.title: Brute Force — Store Values and Rebuild
approaches.intuition: Traverse the list, store values of odd and even indexed nodes separately, then rewrite the list with odd values first followed by even values.
steps: Traverse list and store odd-index values in array.
Store even-index values in another array.
Traverse again and overwrite node values.
cons: Does not preserve node structure (modifies values only)

approaches.title: Improved — Collect Nodes Instead of Values
approaches.intuition: Instead of storing values, store references to odd and even nodes, then reconnect them.
steps: Traverse and store odd nodes in array.
Store even nodes in another array.
Reconnect odd nodes followed by even nodes.

approaches.title: Optimal — In-Place Pointer Manipulation
approaches.intuition: Maintain two chains: one for odd nodes and one for even nodes. Traverse once, rewire pointers, then connect odd chain to even chain.
steps: Initialize odd = head, even = head.next.
Store evenHead for later connection.
Rewire odd.next and even.next alternately.
Attach even list after odd list.

linked-list-cycle-ii.title: Linked List Cycle II
linked-list-cycle-ii.description: Given the head of a linked list, return the node where the cycle begins. If there is no cycle, return null. There is a cycle in the linked list if there is some node that can be reached again by continuously following the next pointer.

examples.explanation: The tail connects to the node with index 1, forming a cycle starting at value 2.
The tail connects to the head node, forming a cycle starting at value 1.
There is no cycle in the linked list.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 10^4].
pos is -1 or a valid index in the linked list.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Nested Traversal
approaches.intuition: For each node, check if it appears again later in the traversal. If a node is revisited, that node is the start of the cycle.
steps: Start from the head node.
For each node, traverse the rest of the list.
If a node appears again, return that node.
If traversal ends without repetition, return null.

approaches.title: Improved — HashSet of Visited Nodes
approaches.intuition: Track all visited nodes in a HashSet. If a node is encountered again, that node is the beginning of the cycle.
steps: Initialize an empty HashSet.
Traverse the linked list.
If a node already exists in the set, return it.
Otherwise, add the node to the set.
If traversal ends, return null.

approaches.title: Optimal — Floyd’s Cycle Detection (Tortoise and Hare)
approaches.intuition: Use two pointers moving at different speeds. If they meet, a cycle exists. Reset one pointer to head and move both one step at a time — the meeting point will be the start of the cycle.
steps: Initialize slow and fast pointers.
Move slow by 1 step and fast by 2 steps.
If they meet, a cycle exists.
Reset slow to head.
Move both pointers one step at a time.
The node where they meet again is the cycle start.

dummy-node.title: Dummy Node Technique
dummy-node.description: Use a dummy node to simplify edge cases in linked list problems, especially when modifications to the head node are possible.

add-two-numbers.title: Add Two Numbers
add-two-numbers.description: You are given two non-empty linked lists representing two non-negative integers. The digits are stored in reverse order, and each of their nodes contains a single digit. Add the two numbers and return the sum as a linked list.
constraints: The number of nodes in each linked list is in the range [1, 100].
It is guaranteed that the list represents a number that does not have leading zeros.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Convert to Numbers
approaches.intuition: Convert both linked lists into full numbers, add them, then convert the result back into a linked list. This works but can overflow for large inputs.
steps: Traverse l1 and compute integer value.
Traverse l2 and compute integer value.
Add the two numbers.
Convert result back into linked list.

approaches.title: Improved — Direct Digit-by-Digit Addition
approaches.intuition: Simulate manual addition from least significant digit. Track carry at each step.
steps: Initialize carry = 0.
Traverse both lists simultaneously.
Add digits and carry.
Create new node with sum % 10.
Update carry = Math.floor(sum / 10).

approaches.title: Optimal — Dummy Node Pattern (Clean Implementation)
approaches.intuition: Use a dummy node to simplify pointer handling. Continue while l1, l2, or carry exists. This is the cleanest and interview-standard implementation.
steps: Create dummy node.
Traverse while l1, l2, or carry exists.
Add digits and carry.
Attach new node.
Return dummy.next.

merge-two-sorted.title: Merge Two Sorted Lists
merge-two-sorted.description: You are given the heads of two sorted linked lists list1 and list2. Merge the two lists into one sorted list and return the head of the merged list.

examples.explanation: Both lists are merged in sorted order.
Both lists are empty.
Merging empty list with [0] gives [0].

constraints: The number of nodes in both lists is in the range [0, 50].
Both list1 and list2 are sorted in non-decreasing order.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Collect Values and Sort
approaches.intuition: Extract all node values from both lists into an array, sort the array, then rebuild a new linked list.
steps: Traverse list1 and push values into array.
Traverse list2 and push values into array.
Sort the combined array.
Build a new linked list from sorted values.
cons: Does not leverage sorted property efficiently

approaches.title: Improved — Iterative Two-Pointer Merge
approaches.intuition: Traverse both lists simultaneously, always choosing the smaller current node. Use a dummy node to simplify pointer handling.
steps: Create dummy node.
Compare list1.val and list2.val.
Attach smaller node to result.
Advance that pointer.
Attach remaining nodes after one list ends.

approaches.title: Optimal — Recursive Merge
approaches.intuition: At each step, choose the smaller head and recursively merge the remaining lists. Very clean and elegant approach.
steps: Base case: if one list is null, return the other.
Compare head values.
Attach smaller node and recursively merge the rest.
cons: May cause stack overflow for very large lists

partition-list.title: Partition List
partition-list.description: Given the head of a linked list and a value x, partition it such that all nodes less than x come before nodes greater than or equal to x. The relative order of nodes in each partition must be preserved.

examples.explanation: All nodes less than 3 appear before nodes greater than or equal to 3, while preserving original relative order.
Node 1 comes before 2 since 1 < 2.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 200].

approaches.title: Brute Force — Collect, Separate, Rebuild
approaches.intuition: Extract all values into two arrays: one for values < x and one for values ≥ x. Then rebuild the linked list from combined arrays.
steps: Traverse list and collect values < x.
Collect values ≥ x.
Rebuild list from concatenated arrays.

approaches.title: Improved — Two Auxiliary Lists (Reuse Nodes)
approaches.intuition: Build two separate linked lists: one for nodes < x and one for nodes ≥ x, using original nodes. Then connect them.
steps: Create two dummy heads: smallDummy and largeDummy.
Traverse original list once.
Attach node to appropriate list.
Connect small list to large list.

approaches.title: Optimal — Clean Dummy Node Pattern
approaches.intuition: This is the cleanest version of the two-list approach. Maintain two chains and stitch them together in-place with constant extra space.
steps: Initialize beforeDummy and afterDummy.
Traverse and attach nodes based on value comparison.
Terminate after list properly.
Connect before list to after list.

rotate-list.title: Rotate List
rotate-list.description: Given the head of a linked list, rotate the list to the right by k places.

examples.explanation:
After rotating 1 step: [5,1,2,3,4]
After rotating 2 steps: [4,5,1,2,3]
k = 4 → 4 % 3 = 1, so rotate 1 step.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 500].

approaches.title: Brute Force — Simulate k Single Rotations
approaches.intuition: Rotate the list one step at a time, repeating k times. Each rotation requires traversing the list to find the last and second-last nodes.
steps: Repeat k times:
Find last node and its previous node.
Detach last node and move it to front.
cons: Too slow when k is large

approaches.title: Improved — Reduce k mod n, Then Simulate
approaches.intuition: Since rotating n times returns the same list, reduce k using k % n. Then simulate only required rotations.
steps: Compute length n.
Reduce k = k % n.
Simulate k rotations.
cons: Still not optimal for large n

approaches.title: Optimal — Circular Link & Break
approaches.intuition: Make the list circular. Then find the new tail at position (n - k % n - 1), break the circle, and return new head.
steps: Compute length n.
Reduce k = k % n.
Connect tail to head (make circular).
Find new tail at (n - k - 1).
Set new head and break circle.

breadcrumb: Delete Node in a Linked List
delete-a-linked-list.title: Delete Node in a Linked List
delete-a-linked-list.description: There is a singly linked list. You are given the node to be deleted. You do not have access to the head of the list. Delete the given node. It is guaranteed that the given node is not the tail node.

examples.explanation: You are given the node with value 5. After deleting it, the list becomes [4,1,9].
You are given the node with value 1. After deleting it, the list becomes [4,5,9].

constraints: The number of nodes in the given list is in the range [2, 1000].
The value of each node in the list is unique.
The given node is not the tail and is guaranteed to exist in the list.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Shift All Values Left
approaches.intuition: Since we cannot access the previous node, shift values from the next nodes backward until the end, then remove the final node.
steps: Copy next node's value into current node.
Move to next node.
Repeat until reaching second-last node.
Set its next pointer to null.

approaches.title: Optimal — Copy-and-Skip (The Trick)
approaches.intuition: Instead of deleting the node directly, copy the next node's value into it, then bypass the next node. This effectively deletes the current node.
steps: Copy node.next.val into node.val.
Set node.next = node.next.next.
cons: Only works because node is guaranteed not to be tail

remove-duplicates-from-sorted-list.title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted List
remove-duplicates-from-sorted-list.description: Given the head of a sorted linked list, delete all duplicates such that each element appears only once. Return the linked list sorted as well.

examples.explanation: Duplicate value 1 is removed.
Duplicate values 1 and 3 are removed.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 300].
The list is guaranteed to be sorted in ascending order.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Hash Set (Ignore Sorted Property)
approaches.intuition: Store seen values in a HashSet. If a value appears again, skip that node.
steps: Create a HashSet to track seen values.
Traverse the list.
If current value exists in set → remove node.
Otherwise add value to set.
pros: Works even if the list is not sorted

approaches.title: Improved — Recursive Deduplication
approaches.intuition: Recursively process the list. If the next node has the same value, skip it.
steps: Base case: if head or head.next is null → return head.
Recursively deduplicate the rest of the list.
If head.val equals head.next.val → skip duplicate.

approaches.title: Optimal — Single-Pass In-Place (Two-Pointer)
approaches.intuition: Because the list is sorted, duplicates appear next to each other. Traverse once and skip nodes with the same value.
steps: Initialize current pointer at head.
While current and current.next exist:
If current.val equals current.next.val → skip next node.
Otherwise move current forward.

recursion.title: Recursion Technique
recursion.description: Use recursion to solve linked list problems by breaking them down into smaller subproblems. This is especially useful for problems that naturally fit a divide-and-conquer approach.

swap-nodes-in-pair.title: Swap Nodes in Pairs
swap-nodes-in-pair.description: Given a linked list, swap every two adjacent nodes and return its head. You must solve the problem without modifying the values in the list's nodes (only nodes themselves may be changed).

examples.explanation: Nodes 1 and 2 are swapped, and nodes 3 and 4 are swapped.
Empty list remains empty.
Single node remains unchanged.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 100].

approaches.title: Brute Force — Array-Based Reconstruction
approaches.intuition: Extract node values into an array, swap adjacent values, then rebuild the linked list.
steps: Traverse list and store values in array.
Swap adjacent elements in array.
Rebuild linked list from modified array.
cons: Violates constraint of not modifying values directly

approaches.title: Improved — Recursive Swapping
approaches.intuition: Swap first two nodes, then recursively swap the rest of the list.
steps: Base case: if less than two nodes, return head.
Swap first two nodes.
Recursively call swap on remaining list.
cons: May overflow stack for very long lists

approaches.title: Optimal — Iterative In-Place Swapping
approaches.intuition: Use a dummy node to simplify pointer manipulation. Iteratively swap pairs by adjusting next pointers.
steps: Create dummy node pointing to head.
Use pointer prev to track node before pair.
Swap two nodes using pointer rewiring.
Move prev forward by two nodes.

palindrom-in-linked-list.title: Palindrome Linked List
palindrom-in-linked-list.description: Given the head of a singly linked list, return true if it is a palindrome or false otherwise.

examples.explanation: The list reads the same forward and backward.
Forward: 1→2, Backward: 2→1.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [1, 10⁵].

approaches.title: Brute Force — Convert to Array
approaches.intuition: Store all node values in an array, then use two pointers to check if the array is a palindrome.
steps: Traverse list and push values into array.
Use left and right pointers to compare values.
Return false if mismatch found.

approaches.title: Improved — Recursive Comparison
approaches.intuition: Use recursion to reach the end of the list, then compare symmetric nodes while unwinding.
steps: Use global/front pointer starting at head.
Recursively move to end of list.
Compare current node with front pointer.
Move front pointer forward.
cons: May cause stack overflow for large lists

approaches.title: Optimal — Reverse Second Half In-Place
approaches.intuition: Use fast and slow pointers to find the middle. Reverse the second half of the list, then compare both halves.
steps: Use slow and fast pointers to find middle.
Reverse second half of list.
Compare first half and reversed second half.
Optionally restore list structure.

breadcrumb: Remove Duplicates from Sorted List II
remove-duplicates-from-sorted-list-2.title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted List II
remove-duplicates-from-sorted-list-2.description: Given the head of a sorted linked list, delete all nodes that have duplicate numbers, leaving only distinct numbers from the original list. Return the linked list sorted as well.

examples.explanation: Values 3 and 4 appear more than once, so they are removed entirely.
Value 1 appears multiple times and is completely removed.
Value 2 appears twice and is removed entirely.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 300].
The list is sorted in non-decreasing order.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Collect Frequencies and Rebuild
approaches.intuition: Count occurrences of each value using a map. Then rebuild a new list including only values that appear exactly once.
steps: Traverse list and count frequency of each value.
Traverse again and build new list with values having frequency 1.
pros: Clear separation of counting and rebuilding

approaches.title: Improved — In-Place with Two Pointers
approaches.intuition: Use a dummy node and two pointers. When duplicates are detected, skip the entire block. Otherwise, link the node to the result chain.
steps: Create dummy node pointing to head.
Use prev pointer to track last valid node.
Use current pointer to scan list.
If current value repeats, skip entire block.
Else, move prev forward.

breadcrumb: Flatten a Multilevel Doubly Linked List
flatten-multilevel-doubly-linked-list.title: Flatten a Multilevel Doubly Linked List
flatten-multilevel-doubly-linked-list.description: You are given a doubly linked list where, in addition to the next and previous pointers, it could have a child pointer, which may point to a separate doubly linked list. These child lists may have one or more children of their own. Flatten the list so that all the nodes appear in a single-level doubly linked list.

examples.explanation: The child list is inserted immediately after the parent node recursively.
No child pointers exist, so list remains unchanged.

constraints: The number of nodes will not exceed 1000.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Collect & Rebuild
approaches.intuition: Traverse the structure using DFS, collect nodes in an array in flattened order, then rebuild the doubly linked list from scratch.
steps: Perform DFS traversal and collect nodes in order.
Create new doubly linked list from collected nodes.
Return rebuilt head.

approaches.title: Improved — Recursive DFS (In-Place Rewiring)
approaches.intuition: Use DFS to flatten child lists recursively. When a node has a child, splice the flattened child list between the node and its next pointer.
steps: Recursively flatten child list.
Connect node.next to flattened child.
Find tail of child list and reconnect original next.
Set child pointer to null.

approaches.title: Optimal — Iterative In-Place Flattening
approaches.intuition: Iterate through list. When encountering a child, find its tail and splice it between current node and next node. No recursion required.
steps: Traverse list with current pointer.
If node has child:
Find tail of child list.
Connect tail to node.next.
Insert child between node and next.
Remove child pointer.

remove-linked-list-elements.title: Remove Linked List Elements
remove-linked-list-elements.description: Given the head of a linked list and an integer val, remove all the nodes of the linked list that have Node.val == val, and return the new head.

examples.explanation: Both nodes with value 6 are removed.
Empty list remains empty.
All nodes are removed.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 10^4].

approaches.title: Brute Force — Create a New List
approaches.intuition: Traverse the list and copy only nodes that do not equal val into a new list.
steps: Create a dummy node for the new list.
Traverse the original list.
If current node value ≠ val → append to new list.
Return new list head.

approaches.title: Improved — In-Place (Handle Head Separately)
approaches.intuition: Modify the list directly, but handle the case where the head node itself must be removed.
steps: Remove leading nodes equal to val.
Traverse remaining list with current pointer.
Skip nodes whose value equals val.
cons: Requires careful handling of head node

approaches.title: Optimal — Dummy Node Technique
approaches.intuition: Use a dummy node before head so that removing the first node becomes identical to removing any other node.
steps: Create dummy node pointing to head.
Use a current pointer starting at dummy.
If current.next.val equals val → skip node.
Otherwise move forward.

in-place-reversal.title: In-Place Reversal Technique
in-place-reversal.description: Reverse linked lists or sublists in-place to solve problems that require reordering nodes. This technique is often used in conjunction with two-pointer approaches.

reverse-a-linked-list.title: Reverse Linked List
reverse-a-linked-list.description: Given the head of a singly linked list, reverse the list and return its head.

examples.explanation: The list is reversed so that the last node becomes the first.
The two nodes are swapped.
An empty list remains empty.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 5000].

approaches.title: Brute Force — Collect & Rebuild
approaches.intuition: Traverse the list, store values in an array, reverse the array, and rebuild a new linked list.
steps: Traverse list and push values into array.
Reverse the array.
Rebuild a new linked list from reversed array.

approaches.title: Improved — Iterative In-Place Reversal
approaches.intuition: Use three pointers: prev, current, next. Reverse the next pointer of each node one by one.
steps: Initialize prev = null.
Iterate through list with current pointer.
Store next node.
Reverse current.next to prev.
Move prev and current forward.

approaches.title: Optimal — Recursive Reversal
approaches.intuition: Reverse the rest of the list recursively, then fix the current node's next pointer.
steps: Base case: if head is null or head.next is null, return head.
Recursively reverse head.next.
Set head.next.next = head.
Set head.next = null.
cons: Risk of stack overflow for large lists

reverse-a-linked-list-ii.title: Reverse Linked List II
reverse-a-linked-list-ii.description: Given the head of a singly linked list and two integers left and right where left ≤ right, reverse the nodes of the list from position left to position right, and return the reversed list.

examples.explanation: Only the sublist [2,3,4] is reversed.
Single node list remains unchanged.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is n.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Extract, Reverse, Rebuild
approaches.intuition: Convert the list to an array, reverse the subarray between left and right, then rebuild the list.
steps: Traverse list and store values in array.
Reverse subarray from left-1 to right-1.
Rebuild linked list from modified array.

approaches.title: Improved — Two-Pass In-Place Reversal
approaches.intuition: First reach the node before 'left'. Then reverse the sublist using standard reversal logic. Finally reconnect both ends.
steps: Use dummy node.
Move prev pointer to node before left.
Reverse sublist from left to right.
Reconnect reversed part.
pros: Clear separation of locating and reversing

approaches.title: Optimal — One-Pass Head-Insertion Method
approaches.intuition: Instead of fully reversing with separate pointers, repeatedly extract the next node and insert it at the front of the sublist. This achieves reversal in one pass.
steps: Use dummy node.
Move prev to node before left.
Let current = prev.next.
Repeat (right - left) times:
Extract next node.
Insert it after prev (front of sublist).

breadcrumb: Reverse Nodes in Even Length Groups
reverse-nodes-in-even-length-group.title: Reverse Nodes in Even Length Groups
reverse-nodes-in-even-length-group.description: You are given the head of a linked list. The nodes are grouped in increasing group sizes: the first group has size 1, the second group has size 2, the third group has size 3, and so on. Reverse the nodes in each group that has an even length. Return the head of the modified list.

examples.explanation:
Group sizes: 1 → [5]
2 → [2,6] (even, reversed)
3 → [3,9,1] (odd, unchanged)
4 → [7,3,8,4] (even, reversed)

Group sizes: 1 → [1]
2 → [1,0] (even, reversed)
Remaining group has size 1 (odd).

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [1, 10⁵].

approaches.title: Brute Force — Extract to Array and Rebuild
approaches.intuition: Extract all values into an array. Process groups by reversing subarrays when group length is even. Then rebuild the linked list.
steps: Traverse list and store values in array.
Process groups of sizes 1,2,3,...
Reverse subarray if group length is even.
Rebuild linked list from modified array.

approaches.title: Improved — Per-Group Node Collection + Value Swap
approaches.intuition: Traverse per group, collect nodes in an array, and if group length is even, swap node values in-place.
steps: Traverse group by group.
Collect nodes in temporary array.
If group length is even, swap node values.
cons: Does not fully use in-place pointer manipulation

approaches.title: Optimal — In-Place Pointer Reversal
approaches.intuition: Traverse group by group. If group length is even, reverse nodes using pointer manipulation. Carefully reconnect previous and next segments.
steps: Use dummy node for safety.
Determine actual group length.
If even, reverse group in-place.
Reconnect segments.

reorder-list.title: Reorder List
reorder-list.description: You are given the head of a singly linked list. Reorder the list to follow the pattern: L0 → Ln → L1 → Ln-1 → L2 → Ln-2 → ... You must modify the list in-place and not return anything.

examples.explanation: Reordered as: 1 → 4 → 2 → 3.
Reordered as: 1 → 5 → 2 → 4 → 3.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [1, 5 * 10⁴].

approaches.title: Brute Force — Store Nodes in Array
approaches.intuition: Store all nodes in an array. Use two pointers from both ends to reconnect nodes in required order.
steps: Traverse list and store nodes in array.
Use left and right pointers.
Reconnect nodes alternately.

approaches.title: Improved — Stack for Second Half
approaches.intuition: Find middle of the list. Push second half nodes onto a stack. Then interleave first half and popped stack nodes.
steps: Find middle using slow-fast pointers.
Push second half nodes into stack.
Merge first half with stack nodes alternately.

approaches.title: Optimal — Split + Reverse + Merge
approaches.intuition: Find middle of list. Reverse second half in-place. Merge two halves alternately.
steps: Find middle using slow-fast pointers.
Reverse second half.
Merge first and reversed second halves alternately.

split-linked-list-in-parts.title: Split Linked List in Parts
split-linked-list-in-parts.description: Given the head of a singly linked list and an integer k, split the linked list into k consecutive parts. The length of each part should be as equal as possible. No two parts should have a size differing by more than one. Earlier parts should have greater or equal size than later parts.

examples.explanation: There are 3 nodes and 5 parts. First 3 parts get one node each; remaining parts are empty.
Total nodes = 10, base size = 3, remainder = 1. First part gets 4 nodes, others get 3.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is in the range [0, 1000].

approaches.title: Brute Force — Extract, Slice, Rebuild
approaches.intuition: Extract all values into an array. Divide array into k slices, then rebuild linked lists for each slice.
steps: Traverse list and store values in array.
Compute base size and remainder.
Slice array into k parts.
Rebuild linked lists from slices.

approaches.title: Improved — Two-Pass with Precomputed Sizes
approaches.intuition: First compute total length. Precompute size of each part in an array. Then iterate through the list and cut links accordingly.
steps: Compute total length n.
Compute sizes array of length k.
Traverse list and split according to sizes.

approaches.title: Optimal — Inline Size Computation + In-Place Split
approaches.intuition: Compute total length. For each part, calculate its size on the fly and split in-place without storing extra arrays.
steps: Compute total length n.
Compute baseSize and remainder.
For each part:
Assign head of part.
Traverse required nodes.
Break link.

reverse-nodes-in-k-group.title: Reverse Nodes in k-Group
reverse-nodes-in-k-group.description: Given the head of a linked list, reverse the nodes of the list k at a time, and return the modified list. If the number of nodes is not a multiple of k, then left-out nodes should remain as is. You must reverse nodes in-place and may not alter the values in the nodes.

examples.explanation: Groups of 2 are reversed. The last node remains unchanged.
First 3 nodes are reversed. Remaining 2 nodes remain unchanged.

constraints: The number of nodes in the list is n.

approaches.title: Brute Force — Array-Based Reconstruction
approaches.intuition: Store all nodes in an array. Reverse every subarray of size k. Then reconnect nodes in new order.
steps: Traverse list and store nodes in array.
For each chunk of size k, reverse it.
Reconnect nodes sequentially.

approaches.title: Improved — Recursive Solution
approaches.intuition: Check if at least k nodes exist. Reverse first k nodes, then recursively process the remaining list and connect.
steps: Check if there are k nodes available.
Reverse first k nodes.
Recursively call for remaining list.
Connect reversed group to recursive result.

approaches.title: Optimal — Iterative In-Place Solution
approaches.intuition: Use a dummy node. For each group, check if k nodes exist. Reverse them in-place and reconnect segments carefully.
steps: Use dummy node pointing to head.
For each group, locate kth node.
Reverse nodes between groupPrev and kth.
Reconnect reversed group.
